title: OLAP Lakehouse: NFR Specification Required
description: Meta-policy pattern for object-storage-centered analytical architectures. Activates when OLAP workload and batch-oriented analytics are both present, and establishes lakehouse tradeoffs versus warehouse-first designs: lower storage cost, open table formats, and flexible data access at the expense of less predictable interactive serving performance. This pattern exists to make lakehouse selection explicit before choosing cloud-native, self-hosted, or SaaS variants.

cost.costNotes: Meta-policy pattern only. Concrete cost is carried by the provider-specific lakehouse variants.

provenance.adoptionCost: Zero adoption cost — this pattern only encodes lakehouse selection posture and NFR interpretation.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost — no runtime infrastructure is provisioned by the meta pattern itself.

provides.reasoning: Makes lakehouse selection explicit for OLAP workloads that are batch-oriented and cost-sensitive, so downstream provider variants can be evaluated against a consistent NFR posture.
Marks the selected analytical architecture as lakehouse-shaped rather than warehouse-first, enabling downstream implementation and planning guidance.

availability.reason: General lakehouse-family policy pattern applicable across cloud-native, self-hosted, and SaaS analytical architectures

supports_nfr.reason: Lakehouse defaults in this registry are optimized for batch-oriented analytics and BI/reporting over object storage, not sub-500ms warehouse-style serving on every cold query.
Lakehouse stacks can reach four nines depending on provider and topology, but this family does not assume five-nines as the default analytical posture.
Lakehouse recovery posture depends on object storage, catalog, and query-service recovery. Five-minute-or-higher RTO is a realistic baseline across the family.
Lakehouse freshness and recoverability are bounded by ingestion and metadata update cadence. Five-minute-or-higher RPO is a realistic baseline across the family.
The default lakehouse variants in this family target low-to-medium BI and analytical concurrency rather than ultra-high concurrent serving.
Lakehouse ingestion is optimized for staged batch writes, micro-batches, or table-format commits rather than very high-frequency row-at-a-time writes.
Lakehouse jobs typically depend on query-service startup, metadata resolution, and batch scheduler orchestration. The family does not assume sub-15-second p95 job starts.
Cold starts, metadata refresh, and queueing can push tail job starts above p95. Thirty seconds is a defensible family-level p99 floor.
Default lakehouse stacks are designed for moderate batch and transformation frequency, not extremely high churn of short-lived jobs.
Lakehouse architectures assume analytical freshness lags source-of-truth writes by at least one ingestion or metadata commit cycle.
Lakehouse analytical stacks are compatible with multiple auth models; access is typically controlled through cloud IAM, service identities, SQL users, or BI-layer federation.
All major lakehouse implementations can operate with or without audit logging depending on governance posture.
Lakehouse platforms can support multiple tenant-isolation models through catalogs, datasets, schemas, row-level controls, or separate workspaces.
PII workloads are possible with the right catalog, access control, encryption, and retention design; the family does not assume either absence or presence of PII.
GDPR compatibility depends on governance and data handling rather than on lakehouse selection alone.
CCPA compatibility depends on lineage, discovery, and deletion workflows rather than on the family choice alone.
HIPAA compatibility depends on provider controls, encryption, logging, and agreements rather than on the family choice alone.
SOX compatibility depends on auditability, access controls, and reporting governance rather than on the family choice alone.
Durability depends on the underlying object storage and catalog design. Both strict and non-strict durability postures are possible within the family.

supports_constraints.reason: Primary gate: lakehouse variants are only relevant for analytical workloads.
Lakehouse architectures are valid across cloud-native and self-hosted environments.
Lakehouse architectures can support analytical access for web, mobile, desktop, and API products, while still acting as the analytical storage and transformation plane.
